title: Dog Heavy Drooling and Nausea: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog drooling excessively and acting nauseous? Learn about potential causes, the urgency level, and how to help your pet safely.
content_html:
TL;DR: Heavy drooling in dogs usually indicates nausea or mouth pain; while typically a medium urgency, it becomes a life-threatening emergency if your dog also has a distended abdomen or unproductive retching.

What are the common causes of hypersalivation and nausea in my dog?

Heavy drooling, medically known as hypersalivation or ptyalism, often occurs in dogs as a direct response to nausea. When a dog feels sick to their stomach, their body produces excess saliva to protect the mouth and throat from potential stomach acid during vomiting. Potential causes include:

  • Dietary indiscretion (eating something they shouldn't)
  • Motion sickness
  • Dental pain or oral injury
  • Anxiety or stress

When is my dog's heavy drooling and nausea considered an emergency?

  • The urgency level is generally classified as Medium, as symptoms may resolve on their own if caused by a minor upset stomach.
  • The urgency increases to High if your dog also experiences a distended abdomen, unproductive retching, or extreme lethargy.
  • These symptoms can signal serious issues like toxin ingestion or a foreign body obstruction.
  • If high-priority symptoms are present, contact an emergency clinic immediately.

How can taking a photo or video help the vet triage my dog?

  • Taking a clear photo of the saliva helps identify its consistency (foamy vs. ropey), color, and any presence of blood.
  • A short video of your dog’s behavior allows a vet to see their posture and facial expressions.
  • These visual clues help a veterinary professional better assess your dog's pain levels and overall stability remotely.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Heavy drooling (ptyalism) and nausea in dogs can be caused by various factors including oral diseases such as foreign bodies, neoplasms, injuries, or mucosal defects; infectious diseases like rabies or distemper; motion sickness, fear, nervousness, or excitement; reluctance to swallow due to esophageal irritation or obstruction, or gastrointestinal stimulation from gastritis or enteritis; sublingual lesions; tonsillitis; medication administration; conformational defects; metabolic disorders like hepatic encephalopathy or uremia; and salivary gland issues. Other potential causes include dietary indiscretion, toxin ingestion, systemic illnesses such as pancreatitis or hypoadrenocorticism, endoparasitism, or bacterial or viral infections. Affected dogs may exhibit depression, anorexia, salivary gland enlargement, weight loss, retching, gagging, regurgitation, vomiting, persistent swallowing, lip smacking, coughing, tachypnea, dyspnea, and abdominal respiration.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Toxicology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 387)

faqs.question: What common toxins cause heavy drooling?
faqs.body: Many common household items like chocolate, certain plants (like lilies or sago palms), and cleaning chemicals can cause immediate nausea and drooling.

faqs.question: Can dental problems cause nausea?
faqs.body: Yes, an infected tooth or an injury to the mouth can cause a dog to drool heavily and act nauseous due to the pain or the bad taste of an infection.

faqs.question: What should I do while monitoring my dog?
faqs.body: Keep your dog in a quiet, cool area. Remove food for a few hours but ensure they have access to fresh water, provided they are not vomiting uncontrollably.
